title: Suresh Malhotra v. Ramesh Gupta
keyArguments: The landlord failed to establish any of the statutory grounds for eviction under the Maharashtra Rent Control Act, 1999
The tenant had been regularly paying rent and the alleged arrears were disputed with valid receipts produced before the court
The landlord's claim of bona fide personal requirement was not substantiated by credible evidence and appeared to be motivated by desire to recover possession for commercial gain
The trial court erred in not appreciating the tenant's long possession of over 22 years and the hardship that eviction would cause
Notice served upon the tenant was procedurally defective and did not comply with the mandatory requirements under Section 16 of the Maharashtra Rent Control Act, 1999
judgmentSummary: The High Court of Bombay allowed the tenant's appeal, setting aside the order of eviction passed by the Small Causes Court, Mumbai. The Court held that the landlord had failed to prove bona fide personal requirement and that the eviction notice was procedurally defective. The tenant's long occupancy and regular payment of rent were recognized as significant factors. The eviction decree was set aside and the tenant's continued possession was confirmed.
judgmentText:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF JUDICATURE AT BOMBAY
ORDINARY ORIGINAL CIVIL JURISDICTION
RENT APPEAL NO. 471 OF 2022

Suresh Malhotra ... Appellant (Original Landlord)
versus
Ramesh Gupta ... Respondent (Original Tenant)

Coram: Hon'ble Justice Priya Nambiar
Date of Judgment: 14th March 2023

JUDGMENT

1. This appeal has been filed by the original landlord, Suresh Malhotra, challenging the order dated 22nd August 2022 passed by the learned Judge, Small Causes Court, Mumbai, whereby the eviction petition filed by the appellant was dismissed. The respondent-tenant, Ramesh Gupta, has been in occupation of the suit premises, being Shop No. 4, Ground Floor, Laxmi Niwas, Dadar (West), Mumbai, since the year 2001 under a registered lease agreement.

2. The appellant had filed an eviction petition before the Small Causes Court on two grounds: first, that the respondent had defaulted in payment of rent for a period of fourteen months, and second, that the appellant required the premises bona fide for his own use and occupation for carrying on the business of his son who had recently returned from abroad.

3. I have carefully perused the records of the trial court and heard the learned counsel for both parties at length. On the first ground of default in payment of rent, the trial court had rightly appreciated that the respondent-tenant produced original rent receipts for the disputed period bearing the signature of the appellant's authorized agent. The appellant failed to rebut the genuineness of these receipts. No handwriting expert was examined to challenge the authenticity of the signatures. In these circumstances, the finding of the trial court that rent was duly paid cannot be interfered with.

4. Regarding the ground of bona fide requirement, the law is well settled that the requirement must be genuine and not a mere pretence to evict a long-standing tenant. The Supreme Court in Gaya Prasad v. Pradeep Srivastava (2001) 2 SCC 604 has reiterated that the burden lies heavily upon the landlord to establish the genuineness of personal need. In the instant case, the appellant's son, who is stated to require the premises, was examined as a witness. However, his testimony revealed that he is presently employed in a private firm in Pune and has no immediate plan to establish a business in Mumbai. There is no documentary evidence such as business plans, bank statements or correspondence to suggest any concrete intention. The requirement projected by the appellant is vague, uncertain and speculative.

5. Furthermore, the eviction notice dated 12th January 2021 issued under Section 16 of the Maharashtra Rent Control Act, 1999 suffers from a material defect. The notice failed to specify the exact ground of eviction with sufficient particularity as mandated under the Act. A defective notice cannot confer jurisdiction upon the court to entertain the eviction petition. This jurisdictional defect alone is sufficient to non-suit the appellant.

6. I further note that the respondent has been in peaceful possession of the suit premises for over twenty-two years, has maintained the property in good condition, and has been conducting a legitimate tailoring business therein. Eviction under such circumstances, particularly when statutory grounds are not made out, would cause irreparable hardship and is contrary to the object and purpose of the Maharashtra Rent Control Act, 1999.

7. In view of the foregoing discussion, I find no merit in the present appeal. The impugned judgment and order dated 22nd August 2022 passed by the learned Judge, Small Causes Court, Mumbai, dismissing the eviction petition, is hereby confirmed. The appeal is dismissed. The respondent-tenant shall continue to be protected in his possession of the suit premises. No order as to costs.

(Justice Priya Nambiar)
High Court of Judicature at Bombay
14th March 2023
